content.full_text:
F ern P olrrrcel P RACTTcEs C ouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95911-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January l7,2OOg
N{ichael Reiter
Assistant City Attorney
City of Redlands
P.O. Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373-1005
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-08-003
Dear Mr. Reiter:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Redlands city
Councilmember Jerry Bean for advice regarding the council member's duties under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that
our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion orr
the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May Councilmember Bean vote to receive and file a consent calendar that
includes an item in which he has a conflict of interest?
CONCLUSION
The councilmember may not make, participate in making or influence that
decision. [n addition, the councilmember must identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed below.
on the record of the meeting. However, Section 87105 does not require the
councilmember to leave the room since the decision is on a consent calendar.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 rhrough 9 I 0 14. All
statutory references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through l1ggT of title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory rel'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-08-003
Page No. 2
FACTS
The City of Redlands Community Development Department regularly submits a
report to the City Council summarizing actions taken by the Planning Commission with
respect to development projects. Customarily, these reports are on de City Council,s
consent calendar, and as part of one motion as to all consented items, the rlport is
"received and filed" by the Council. There is no discussion of the developei application
or action taken by the Council on the developer application.
The council member asks for advice on the following situation: If one of the
items within the consent calendar concerns planning commiision action on a developer's
application, and the developer is a source of income to the council member as defined in
the Act, would the council member have a conflict of interest in making or participating
in a motion to "receive and file" the consent calendar?
ANALYSN
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in, or using his
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public
official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision "if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguisha6le from its
effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her im-mediate family,
or on any" of the official's economic interests.
To determine whether a public official has such a "conflict of interest,' in a
governmental decision, the Commission has developed a standard, eight-step analysis
outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of Regulation iS7OO(b). Regulation taZOOlbi
provides:
"(b) To determine whether a given individual has a disqualifying conflict
of interest under the political Reform Act, proceed with the foilJwing
analysis:
"(1) Determine whether the individual is a public official, within the
meaning of the Act. (See Government code section gzo4g;2 cal. code
Regs. $ 18701.) If the individual is not a public official, he or she does
not have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the political Reform
Act.
"(2) Determine whether the public official will be making, participating in
making, or using or attempting to use his/her official poritio, to influence
a government decision. (See 2 Cal. code Regs. $ 1g702.) If the public
official is not making, participating in making, or using or attempting to


File No. 4-08-003
Page No. 3
use his/her official position to influence a government decision, then he or
she does not have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the political
Reform Act.
*(3) Identify the public official's economic interests. (See 2 Cal. code
Regs. g 18703.)
"(4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine
whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision which the public official will be making, participatiirg in making,
9 51"g or attempting to use his/her official position to influence. (see 2
Cal. Code Regs. $ 18704.)
"(5) Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic
inter-est, based upon the degree of involvement determined pursuant to
California code of Regulations, title 2, section lg7o4. (See 2 Cal. code
Regs. $ 18705.)
"(6) Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental
decision will have a material financial effect (as defined in California
Code of Regulations, title 2, section 1s705) on each economic interest
identified pursuant to califomia code of Regulations, title 2, section
18703. (See 2 Cal. Code Regs. $ 1g706.) If it is not reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on any of the
public official's economic interests, he or she does not have a conflict of
interest within the meaning of the political Reform Act. If it is reasonabry
foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on any of the
public official's economic interests, and the official does not participate in
the decision, determine whether the official may segment the decision into
separate decisions to allow his or her participution i, subsequent
decisions. (See 2 Cal. Code Regs. $ 1g709.)-
"(7) Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is
distinguishable from the effect on the pubric generalry. If the official can
establish that the reasonably foreseeable material finincial effect on his or
her economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, he or she does not have a conflict of interest within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act. If the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the public official's economic interest is distinguishable
from the effect on the pubric generally, he or she has a conflict of interest
y:fh_,1the meaning of the political Reform Acr. (see 2 cal.code Regs. g
18707.)


File No. A-08-003
Page No.4
"(8) Determine if the public official's participation is legally required
despite the conflict of interest. If the officiaf can establiih tt ut tii, or her
participation is legally required, he or she may participate in the
governmental decision despite the conflict of inierest. (See 2 Cal. Code
Regs. g 18708.)"
You acknowledged that the councilmember is a "public official', and that he has
an economic interest in an applicant with a proposal that will be presented to the city
council. Your question is whether the couniilmember is permitted to vote to ,,receive
and file" the report including the planning commission's actions taken regarding the
source of income.
- 
Assuming the financial interest in the decision exists, the Act prohibits
Councilmember Bean from making, participating in or influencing the govemmental
decisions. In addition, since the councilmembeiis a public official whose office is listed
in Section 87200, then pursuant to Section 87105 and Regulation 1g702.5, he must
immediately prior to the discussion of the item: (l) identiiy each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economicinterest, as discussed
in Regulation 18702.5(b)(1XB),2 on the record of the meeting and(2)recuse himself
from any participation or discussion of the item. However, Section g7105 does not
require the councilmember to leave the room so long as the matter has been placed on the
P-ortign of the agenda reserved for uncontested mattJrs. Regulation 1g702.5taXrl
clarifies:
"The exception from leaving the room granted in Government code
section 87105(aX3) for a 'matter [that] has been placed on the portion of
the agenda reserved for uncontested matters' shail mean agenda items on
the consent calendar. When the matter in which the public official has a
financial interest is on the consent calendar, the pubiic official must
comply with subdivisions (bxl) and (b)(2) of this regulation, and recuse
himself or herself from discussing or voting on that matter, but the public
official is not required to leave the room ouring the consent calendar.,,
You note that the Jeffries Advice Letter, No. 4-99-032 provides a different
conclusion. However, this letter predated the express language provided in Section
87105 and Regularion 18702.5.
2 Regulation 18702-.5(b)( l)(B) requires the following: "(i) if an investment, rhe name of the
business entity in which each investment ii treta; (ii) if a urrl"r"rr'poriiion, u g"r"rul description of the
business activity in which the business entity is engaged as wello, th. nur. of the businesi entity; (iii) if
real property, the address or another indication o.f the location of the property, unless the property is the
p.ublic-official's principal or personal residence, in which case, identihcationthat tt. prnp..ti is a residence
(iv) if income or gifts, then identification of the source; and (v) if personal financial effect, then
identification of the expense, Iiabirity, asset or income affected.,'


File No. 4,-08-003
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322_5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division
Jwwjgl
